lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market commercial and institutional general contractors, design-build firms, and project construction-management firms that repeatedly deliver new construction, additions, alterations, maintenance, or repairs for external private, nonprofit, and public customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Specialty trades classified outside 236220; owner-captive construction units; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and for-sale/speculative builders whose activity is not an outsourced service to an external customer.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based revenue from commercial, institutional, and public owners under competitively bid or negotiated fixed-price, guaranteed-maximum-price, cost-plus, design-build, and construction-management arrangements; repeat owners, frameworks, renovations, and follow-on projects provide repeat-service economics despite finite individual projects.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2023 occupation and wage mix for NAICS 236200, which combines 236220 with industrial-building construction, rather than task-level AI exposure for the exact lens. I qualitatively wage-weight higher exposure in management, project management, estimating, finance, and office support and low exposure in field trades, while using RICS evidence to remove already-embedded use. This can overstate substitution where site context and professional judgment dominate, or understate it if multimodal takeoff and project-document tools improve rapidly.
a.rationale: In broader nonresidential building construction, 52.35% of workers are in construction and extraction occupations, versus 15.16% in management, 12.70% in business and financial operations, and 7.65% in office support. Construction managers represent 9.30% of employment, project-management specialists 7.25%, and cost estimators 2.29%, concentrating wage dollars in document-heavy tasks such as takeoffs, bidding, scheduling, RFIs, submittals, procurement, reporting, and accounting. RICS reports only about 15% active AI use and 78% either no implementation or pilots, so most modeled exposure remains not yet automated.
a.change_evidence: A 236220 employer time-and-motion and payroll study mapping wage dollars to estimating, project controls, RFIs, submittals, scheduling, procurement, safety documentation, accounting, business development, and physical site execution, paired with audited pre/post AI staffing hours.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 236200 and 2023 vintage, though 236220 is a major component of that group.
The task-exposure weights are bounded judgment rather than an observed automation measure.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and was harmonized across industries, potentially obscuring current project and subcontracting mix.

rho.bridge: RICS surveys global built-environment professionals and AGC surveys US commercial contractors on present adoption and investment plans, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor opportunity for LMM 236220 firms. I translate low present readiness and rising investment into an implementation fraction; the global sample may understate US use, while stated investment and tool use may overstate completed workflow redesign and payroll effects.
rho.rationale: RICS found 45% of organizations had no AI implementation, 34% were in pilots, about 15% were active users, and 74% had limited or no preparation. In contrast, AGC's 2026 US survey reports 61% use AI or plan to increase investment, with use concentrated in office/admin (45%), estimating (23%), and design/preconstruction (20%). Five years permits diffusion through standardized office and project-document workflows, but fragmented data, changing project teams, owner requirements, quality assurance, liability, integration cost, and shortages of AI-skilled staff prevent complete implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of LMM 236220 firms reporting AI use by workflow, share of live projects covered, human exception rates, rework and claims, governance approval, and labor hours or avoided hires after at least two project cycles.
caveats: Only 10% of the RICS respondent base came from the Americas.
AGC combines current use with plans to increase investment and does not measure labor substitution.
Public procurement, owner technology mandates, safety rules, and project-specific data structures can slow implementation.

e.bridge: The NAICS manual establishes that 236220 includes external-service contractor models but also for-sale builders, while an Ontario ICI survey measures work mix rather than the share of US LMM firms eligible under the lens. I use the survey's 73% overall repeat-customer work and 67% among general contractors as evidence of repeat outsourced relationships, then adjust for speculative builders and one-off or owner-dependent firms. The Canadian mixed-contractor population may differ in either direction from US 236220.
e.rationale: General contracting, design-build, and construction management for outside building owners fit the outsourced-service lens, and repeat-customer work is common in the closest available contractor survey. Eligibility is reduced for speculative/for-sale builders, firms effectively captive to one developer, and project businesses without a transferable organization independent of the owner.
e.change_evidence: A US 236220 firm-level survey or transaction database tagging external-customer revenue, repeat-customer percentage, speculative development, customer concentration, public/private mix, and management depth for firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
caveats: Repeat-customer revenue is not a direct firm-eligibility measure.
The survey is Ontario-based and its sample is 80% trade contractors, although it reports a general-contractor split.
The exact six-digit share of for-sale builders and owner-dependent firms is unavailable.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA surveys almost 300 engineering-and-construction executives on intended exits, while Gallup surveys US employer-business owners across industries on five-year sale or transfer plans. I reduce FMI's 38% planned-exit share for noncompletion, liquidation, non-control changes, and timing outside the window, and use Gallup's 22% employer plan rate as a lower cross-check. FMI's larger-company skew may increase transition formality, while LMM owner dependence may increase succession pressure but reduce saleability.
s5.rationale: FMI/CFMA found 38% of E&C owners intended to exit in fewer than five years, yet 51% of that group lacked a defined ownership-transfer plan and 64% of respondents intended internal transitions. Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years. A one-quarter probability allows for real succession pressure but excludes closures and recognizes that many intentions will not become qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year linked PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, broker, and licensing records for US 236220 firms in the target EBITDA band, coded for closed third-party control sale, internal control transfer, closure, owner age, bonding continuity, and no event.
caveats: Exit plans do not equal closed qualifying transfers.
FMI/CFMA covers broad E&C and firms above the target band; 44% of respondents were below $20 million in revenue.
Licensing, bonding, customer relationships, and key-person dependence can make a nominally available firm nontransferable.

q.rationale: A contractor can retain labor savings inside an existing firm-fixed-price or guaranteed-price project because it bears performance-cost risk. Cost-plus, open-book construction-management, public work, and fee-based arrangements expose cost savings to the owner, and competitive rebids progressively incorporate lower delivery costs. Prequalification, schedule reliability, scarce staff, customer trust, and the ability to pursue more work preserve some value, but sophisticated owners and transparent procurement limit long-run retention.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level backlog analysis for representative LMM 236220 firms by fixed-price, GMP, cost-plus, fee, public-bid, and negotiated delivery, linked to bid spreads, win rates, renewal pricing, and gross-margin retention after AI deployment.
caveats: No observed source measures discounted five-year AI-benefit retention for 236220.
The code spans public institutions, negotiated private work, and highly competitive bid markets with different pass-through dynamics.
The estimate excludes demand, implementation cost, and implementation feasibility by design.

d5.bridge: Census reports nominal construction spending by building category, and AIA forecasts nominal nonresidential sectors only through 2027, not constant-price five-year demand for the full 236220 service basket. I combine commercial and institutional directions, recognize that data centers are explicitly included in 236220, deflate directionally, and extend a normalized scenario through year five. Nominal forecasts overstate real quantity under construction inflation, while Census category spending may include work delivered by firms outside the target lens.
d5.rationale: The current market is uneven rather than uniformly weak. Census reports May 2026 total nonresidential spending 3.8% below May 2025 and private nonresidential down 6.6%. AIA forecasts commercial spending up 4.8% in 2026 and 5.8% in 2027, largely from data centers, while institutional spending rises 2.8% and 2.7%; without data centers, commercial would fall 1% in 2026 and rise only 1% in 2027. A diverse base of healthcare, schools, public safety, entertainment, hotels, offices, warehouses, renovations, and fast-growing data centers supports roughly stable five-year constant-price quantity, with wide cyclical bounds.
d5.change_evidence: A 2027-2031 Dodge, ConstructConnect, FMI, or AIA forecast in constant dollars for all 236220 building categories, split between new build and alteration/repair and mapped to LMM general-contractor revenue rather than total put-in-place spending.
caveats: The public forecasts extend only 12-18 months; years three through five are judgmental.
Construction spending is nominal and the real-quantity conversion is sensitive to labor and materials inflation.
Data-center growth is unusually concentrated, while office, warehouse, retail, lodging, education, and regional markets can diverge sharply.

o.rationale: Commercial and institutional construction is a physical, site-specific service with contractual, safety, schedule, quality, subcontractor, and regulatory accountability. AI and software can reduce design-coordination, takeoff, documentation, and project-control labor but cannot independently contract for and deliver a building, coordinate site trades and materials, resolve physical exceptions, or bear completion liability. Modular construction and owner self-performance can displace part of the operator's scope, but nearly all remaining quantity should still require an accountable operator of the lens's kind.
o.change_evidence: Owner procurement and building-permit data measuring direct owner self-performance, modular or off-site turnkey delivery, autonomous site construction, and software-only delivery shares, alongside insurer, surety, and licensing evidence about transfer of accountable-contractor functions.
caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share five years ahead.
The code includes modular on-site assembly, and further prefabrication could reduce on-site scope without removing overall accountability.
Large public agencies, institutions, retailers, and data-center owners may internalize more construction management than assumed.

narrative.executive_view: Commercial and institutional builders have a bounded AI opportunity concentrated in estimating, project controls, procurement, reporting, and administration rather than physical construction. The addressable firm population is large and repeat relationships are common, but competition, public procurement, owner transparency, bonding, and project cyclicality limit how automatically operational efficiency becomes retained value.
narrative.ai_impact: The most practical applications are takeoffs, bid leveling, schedule and cost-risk review, contract search, RFIs and submittals, progress documentation, compliance drafting, accounting, and business development. Field execution, supervision, safety judgment, owner negotiation, and exception handling remain human-intensive; fragmented data and changing project teams make scaling harder than isolated tool demos suggest.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price and guaranteed-price work can retain savings during a project, but cost-plus and fee arrangements reveal them to owners. Public bidding and private competitive tendering should reprice part of any recurring cost advantage, while prequalification, speed, reliability, and scarce project-management capacity can preserve a share through better throughput and selective bidding.
narrative.firm_availability: Most general contracting, design-build, and construction-management firms serve external owners and can earn repeat work, although the code also includes speculative builders and owner-dependent project businesses. Succession evidence suggests meaningful transfer activity, but many owners lack plans, favor internal transitions, or operate firms whose bonding and customer relationships do not survive a control change cleanly.
narrative.demand_durability: The service remains strongly operator-required because buildings must be physically delivered under accountable safety, contract, code, and schedule management. Quantity demand is diversified but uneven: data centers and healthcare are strong, while traditional offices, warehouses, and some discretionary commercial segments remain soft; institutional and renovation work add stability.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The screen can fail if it treats AI assistance as headcount elimination, assumes all contractors have usable project data, or overlooks local cyclicality and public-bid pricing. Customer concentration, fixed-price overruns, claims, bonding limits, subcontractor failures, loss of key estimators or superintendents, and the highly varied building-category mix can dominate any modeled savings.
narrative.principal_driver: AI-assisted preconstruction and project administration can stretch scarce managerial capacity across a large base of repeat, externally served building owners.
narrative.principal_weakness: Competitive and transparent contracting can pass savings to customers, while demand is fragmented across sharply diverging building categories and regions.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 236200
sources.supports: Nonresidential-building occupation mix: 52.35% construction/extraction, 15.16% management, 12.70% business/financial, and 7.65% office support, with detailed employment and wages for construction managers, project-management specialists, and estimators.

sources.title: RICS artificial intelligence in construction report 2025
sources.supports: Global survey of more than 2,200 construction professionals: 45% no implementation, 34% pilots, about 15% active use, 74% limited/no preparation, plus adoption barriers and five-year workflow expectations.

sources.title: The 2026 Construction Hiring and Business Outlook
sources.supports: US commercial-contractor survey reports 61% use AI or plan increased investment, with 45% using it for office/admin, 23% for estimating, and 20% for design/preconstruction; also documents labor and project constraints.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022: 236220 definition
sources.supports: Defines 236220 as new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs for commercial and institutional buildings, including general contractors, for-sale builders, design-build firms, construction managers, modular assembly, data centers, hospitals, schools, offices, hotels, and warehouses.

sources.title: 2026 Ontario Construction Secretariat Contractor Survey
sources.supports: Survey of 400 ICI contractors reports 73% of work from repeat customers overall and 67% for general contractors, along with bid/sole-source mix, maintenance work, and prequalification evidence.

sources.title: 2024 Ownership Transfer & Management Succession: FMI and CFMA Industry Study
sources.supports: Survey of almost 300 E&C executives: 38% planned exit in fewer than five years, 51% of that group lacked a defined transfer plan, 64% intended internal transition, and 44% represented firms below $20 million revenue.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey shows 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% were unsure or had no plan.

sources.title: Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 16: Types of Contracts
sources.supports: Explains firm-fixed-price contractor responsibility for performance costs and profit/loss versus minimal cost responsibility under cost-plus-fixed-fee, and notes sealed bids use fixed-price forms; supports value-retention mechanisms.

sources.title: Monthly Construction Spending, May 2026
sources.supports: Census table reports total nonresidential construction spending down 3.8% year over year and private nonresidential down 6.6%, with current levels and changes across office, commercial, healthcare, education, lodging, and other relevant categories.

sources.title: July 2026 Consensus Construction Forecast
sources.supports: AIA panel forecasts commercial spending up 4.8% in 2026 and 5.8% in 2027, institutional up 2.8% and 2.7%, and data centers up 33% and 25%; without data centers, commercial is forecast down 1% then up 1%.
